name: Poem: "On Killing a Tree" — Gieve Patel

sourceRefs.label: civilserviceaspirants.in - MCQ set (figures of speech per line; one item discarded as mismatched site content)
poemanalysis.com, poemhunter.com - full poem text, two-part structure, imagery of the exposed root and final death-stage
en.wikipedia.org, homegrown.co.in, poemist.com - Gieve Patel biography (physician, Green Movement writer, other poems/plays)

researchNotes: OCR index search for 'killing a tree'/'gieve patel' returned zero hits — web research only. One sourced 'question' was discarded outright: the source page's Q1 quoted Robert Frost's 'Stopping by Woods' stanza under this poem's heading, a clear site content-mixing error — caught by recognizing the quoted lines didn't match this poem at all, not used. The remaining 5 questions are genuinely about this poem and were kept. Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned OCR again (still zero hits, confirmed 100% web-research), sourced the full poem text (the original pass only quoted lines from its first stanza), and added the poem's two-part argument structure (superficial harm heals vs. root-removal is required, followed by the clinical final death-stage stanza), close readings of the tree's nourishment imagery and the exposed root's personification, the title's ironic 'how-to manual' tone, and real biographical facts about Gieve Patel (a practicing physician and 'Green Movement' writer, also known for other poems/plays) that were entirely missing from the original pass.

reading.intro: A poem arguing that truly killing a tree takes far more than a single cut — it requires destroying its root, the hidden source of its life. Real coaching-site evidence (after discarding one mismatched/wrong-poem question found on the source page) tests figure-of-speech identification on specific lines.

qa.question: What is the poem's central argument?
qa.answer: It takes much more effort to truly kill a tree than a single jab or cut. The tree has grown slowly, absorbing years of sunlight, air, and water — cutting and bruising it isn't enough, since it will heal and grow again. The root, hidden underground, must be exposed and fully destroyed for the tree to actually die.

qa.question: What is the deeper/metaphorical meaning some readings draw from the poem?
qa.answer: The poem can be read as being about confronting deep-seated feelings or habits — superficial treatment (a quick cut) doesn't resolve something with deep roots; only fully exposing and addressing the root cause truly 'kills' it.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "It has grown / Slowly consuming the earth."
qa.answer: Personification — the tree is given the active, almost intentional action of 'consuming' the earth, as though it were a living agent in pursuit of nourishment.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "leperous hide" (describing the tree's bark).
qa.answer: Metaphor — the bark is directly described as 'leperous hide' (skin), without 'like/as', comparing its rough, scarred texture to a disease-marked skin.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "The bleeding bark will heal."
qa.answer: Personification — the bark is described as capable of 'bleeding' and 'healing', human/animal-like properties applied to plant tissue.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "It is to be roped, tied, / And pulled out — snapped out / Or pulled out entirely."
qa.answer: Repetition — 'pulled out' is repeated, emphasizing the forceful, thorough effort needed to fully remove the root.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "Out of the anchoring earth."
qa.answer: Metaphor — the earth is described as 'anchoring', directly likening it to an anchor holding the root in place, without 'like/as'.

qa.question: What is the poem's overall two-part structure?
qa.answer: Part one describes superficial violence (a knife jab, hacking, chopping) that FAILS to kill the tree, since the bark heals and new twigs regrow from close to the ground. Part two describes the only method that actually works — the root must be roped, tied, and pulled out entirely, exposing it to the earth's surface. A short final section then describes the actual death process once the root is exposed: scorching, browning, hardening, twisting, withering.

qa.question: What imagery describes how the tree has grown, in the poem's opening lines, and what does it emphasize?
qa.answer: "Rising out of it [the earth], feeding / Upon its crust, absorbing / Years of sunlight, air, water" — vivid imagery of the tree's slow, patient nourishment from earth, sun, air, and water over many years, emphasizing just how much time and resource went into the tree's growth, which is why killing it properly requires an equally deliberate, significant effort.

qa.question: How is the tree's exposed root described once pulled from the ground, and what technique does this use?
qa.answer: "The strength of the tree exposed, / The source, white and wet, / The most sensitive, hidden / For years inside the earth." This uses personification/vivid imagery — the root is described almost like an exposed nerve or vital organ ('the most sensitive'), emphasizing its vulnerability once removed from its protective, hidden position underground.
qa.tip: Fast personification check across this whole poem: whenever a plant part is given a human/animal-body-like quality (bark that 'bleeds' and 'heals', a root that is 'sensitive', earth that 'anchors') rather than just a plain physical description, that's personification.

qa.question: What is the tone of the poem's final stanza ("scorching and choking... browning, hardening, twisting, withering, and then it is done"), and how does this tone relate to the poem's title?
qa.answer: The tone is clinical, detached, and almost matter-of-fact — listing the tree's death stages like a technical process rather than a tragedy. This detached, procedural tone echoes the poem's title, 'On Killing a Tree', which itself reads almost like a how-to manual — an ironic technique, since a title that sounds like practical instructions is actually describing something genuinely violent and drawn-out.

qa.question: What is Gieve Patel's biographical background, and what literary movement was he associated with?
qa.answer: Gieve Patel (1940-2023) was an Indian poet, playwright, painter, AND a practicing physician (he worked as a general practitioner in Mumbai until his retirement in 2005). He was associated with the 'Green Movement' of writers concerned with environmental protection — his poems consistently express deep concern for nature and expose human cruelty toward it.

qa.question: Name two other notable poems or plays by Gieve Patel, besides "On Killing a Tree".
qa.answer: Poems: 'How Do You Withstand' (1966), 'Body' (1976), 'Mirrored, Mirroring' (1991). Plays: 'Princes' (1971), 'Savaksa' (1982), 'Mr. Behram' (1987).

qa.question: What technique is used in "So hack and chop / But this alone won't do it," and what point does it make?
qa.answer: Short, blunt, almost monosyllabic verbs ('hack,' 'chop') mimic the physical act of attacking the tree, immediately followed by a flat rejection ('won't do it') — reinforcing the poem's central argument that superficial violence is futile against something as resilient as a tree.

qa.question: Beyond trees, what does the poem's underlying logic imply about resilience and destruction more broadly?
qa.answer: Many readings extend the poem's logic metaphorically: truly ending or changing something deeply rooted — a habit, a belief, an entrenched system — requires addressing its hidden root cause, not just its visible surface. A quick or superficial fix allows the same thing to regenerate, just as a tree regrows from a merely hacked trunk.

questions.prompt: According to the poem, what is required to TRULY kill a tree (not just cut it)?
questions.explanation: The root, hidden underground, must be exposed and destroyed — a cut alone allows the tree to heal and regrow.

questions.prompt: "It has grown / Slowly consuming the earth" uses which figure of speech?
questions.explanation: Personification — the tree given the active action of 'consuming'.

questions.prompt: "Leperous hide" describing the tree's bark is an example of:
questions.explanation: Metaphor — bark directly described as diseased skin.

questions.prompt: "The bleeding bark will heal" uses which figure of speech?
questions.explanation: Personification — bark given the ability to 'bleed' and 'heal'.

questions.prompt: The repeated phrase "pulled out" in the lines about the root is an example of:
questions.explanation: Repetition — emphasizing the forceful effort needed.

questions.prompt: "Out of the anchoring earth" uses which figure of speech?
What is the poem's overall two-part structure?
options: Superficial harm (heals) vs. root removal (kills), then the death process
A happy stanza followed by a sad stanza
A question followed by an answer
Four unrelated short poems about trees
questions.explanation: Part one shows superficial violence failing (the tree heals); part two shows root removal succeeding, followed by a final stanza describing the actual death process.

questions.prompt: "Rising out of it, feeding / Upon its crust, absorbing / Years of sunlight, air, water" primarily uses which technique, and to what end?
options: Imagery, to emphasize how much time/resource the tree's growth took
Hyperbole, to exaggerate the tree's age
Simile, comparing the tree to a person eating
questions.explanation: Vivid imagery of years of slow nourishment, justifying why killing the tree properly requires equally deliberate effort.

questions.prompt: "The source, white and wet, / The most sensitive, hidden / For years inside the earth" describes the exposed root using which technique?
options: Personification/imagery (root as an exposed nerve-like organ)
questions.explanation: The root is described almost like an exposed nerve or vital organ, emphasizing its vulnerability once removed from the earth.
questions.trick: Across this poem, any plant part given a human/animal-body-like quality (bleeding bark, a sensitive root, anchoring earth) is personification — a fast, reusable check for every 'identify the figure of speech' line in this poem.

questions.prompt: The tone of the poem's final stanza (scorching, browning, hardening, withering) is best described as:
options: Clinical and detached, echoing the title's 'how-to manual' irony
questions.explanation: A clinical, procedural tone that ironically echoes the title's instructional-sounding phrasing.

questions.prompt: Besides being a poet, what was Gieve Patel's profession, and what literary movement was he part of?
questions.explanation: A practicing physician in Mumbai, and part of the environmentally-concerned 'Green Movement' of writers.

questions.prompt: Which of these is another notable poem by Gieve Patel?
questions.explanation: "How Do You Withstand" (1966) — the others are by different poets already covered elsewhere in this syllabus.

questions.prompt: "So hack and chop / But this alone won't do it" uses short, blunt, monosyllabic verbs to:
options: Mimic the physical act of attacking the tree, then flatly reject its effectiveness
questions.explanation: The blunt verbs mimic violence, immediately undercut by the flat rejection that superficial attack 'won't do it'.

questions.prompt: Extended metaphorically beyond trees, the poem's central argument suggests that truly changing something deeply rooted (a habit, a belief, a system) requires:
options: Addressing its hidden root cause directly
Waiting for it to disappear on its own
questions.explanation: Just as a hacked tree trunk regrows, a superficial fix allows a deep-rooted problem to regenerate — only addressing the root cause truly resolves it.

questions.prompt: "On Killing a Tree" is written in which form?
options: Free verse, without a fixed rhyme scheme
questions.explanation: Free verse — no fixed rhyme scheme or meter, structured instead around its two-part argument.

questions.prompt: The poem's title, "On Killing a Tree", reads almost like the title of a how-to manual. What effect does this create?
options: It makes the poem feel lighthearted throughout
An ironic contrast between the procedural-sounding title and the poem's genuinely violent, drawn-out subject
It confirms the poem is a literal gardening guide
questions.explanation: The clinical, instructional-sounding title ironically contrasts with the poem's grim, extended depiction of killing something resilient.

questions.prompt: Approximately how many verse-paragraphs (stanzas) make up "On Killing a Tree"?
questions.explanation: Four verse-paragraphs of varying, irregular length, typical of the poem's free-verse form.
